content.text: Not all agent runs have the same urgency. A customer-facing chatbot answering a live question competes for workers with a nightly batch job summarising last month's tickets. Without priority queues, the batch job can starve the live request. Priority queues give every tier its own queue; workers drain higher-priority queues first, so low-latency workloads get processed regardless of how much batch work is waiting.

attrs.alt: Priority queue architecture: four tier queues (critical/high/normal/batch) fed by Redis sorted sets, drained by a worker pool that polls in priority order

content.text: Define tiers against concrete SLAs, not vague importance. Workers enforce these SLAs by draining tiers in order.
Use one Redis sorted set per tier. The score is the
(Unix ms) so that within a tier, jobs are processed FIFO.
blocks until an item arrives and atomically removes the lowest-score member — no separate locking needed.

on each queue in priority order with a zero timeout to check without blocking, then falls back to a blocking wait on the highest non-empty tier. This avoids busy-looping while still draining higher tiers before lower ones.
Strict priority can starve low-priority jobs indefinitely if high-priority load is constant.

solves this by periodically promoting old low-priority jobs: their Redis score is subtracted by an aging bonus proportional to their wait time, making them appear "older" than their real enqueue time and eligible for earlier dequeue.

AGING_INTERVAL_S  = 30    # run aging every 30 seconds
AGING_BONUS_PER_S = 0.5  # move score backward 0.5s per second waited
PROMOTION_THRESHOLD_S = 120  # promote batch → normal after 2 min

def age_queue(priority: Priority) -> int:
    """
    Subtract aging bonus from scores of old entries to boost their
    effective priority within the same tier.
    Returns number of entries aged.
    """
    now   = time.time()
    items = r.zrangebyscore(QUEUE_KEY[priority], "-inf", "+inf", withscores=True)
    aged  = 0
    pipe  = r.pipeline()
    for payload, score in items:
        wait_s = now - score
        if wait_s > 60:  # only age jobs waiting more than 60s
            new_score = score - (wait_s * AGING_BONUS_PER_S)
            pipe.zadd(QUEUE_KEY[priority], {payload: new_score}, xx=True)
            aged += 1
    pipe.execute()
    return aged


def promote_batch_to_normal() -> int:
    """Move batch jobs older than PROMOTION_THRESHOLD_S to normal queue."""
    cutoff = time.time() - PROMOTION_THRESHOLD_S
    old_items = r.zrangebyscore(QUEUE_KEY[Priority.BATCH], "-inf", cutoff, withscores=True)
    pipe = r.pipeline()
    for payload, score in old_items:
        pipe.zrem(QUEUE_KEY[Priority.BATCH], payload)
        pipe.zadd(QUEUE_KEY[Priority.NORMAL], {payload: score})
    pipe.execute()
    return len(old_items)
An alternative to strict priority is weighted allocation: dedicate a fraction of workers to each tier so batch work always makes some progress, even when higher tiers have load.
exceeds the tier's SLA target. A
job waiting more than 5 seconds indicates either worker starvation or a bug in the polling loop.
Choosing Between Strict Priority and Weighted Allocation
Interactive + batch mix with clear SLA separation
When batch work must always progress
In a multi-tenant system, one busy tenant can dominate a tier's queue even with priority tiers in place. Within a tier, use
: each tenant gets a per-tenant sub-queue and workers round-robin across tenants rather than draining the full tier FIFO.
This approach gives each active tenant at most
of the tier's capacity where N is the number of active tenants. A tenant who submits 100 jobs cannot delay another tenant's single job by more than N polling rounds.
Unbounded queues can exhaust memory during traffic spikes. Enforce a maximum depth per tier and return an HTTP 429 with a
header when the queue is full.

MAX_QUEUE_DEPTH = {
    Priority.CRITICAL: 1_000,
    Priority.HIGH:     5_000,
    Priority.NORMAL:  20_000,
    Priority.BATCH:   100_000,
}

class QueueFullError(Exception):
    def __init__(self, priority: Priority, depth: int):
        self.priority = priority
        self.depth    = depth
        super().__init__(f"Queue {priority.value} full ({depth} items)")


def enqueue_with_backpressure(job: AgentJob, priority: Priority) -> None:
    max_depth = MAX_QUEUE_DEPTH[priority]
    key       = QUEUE_KEY[priority]

    # Lua script: atomic check + add
    ENQUEUE_SCRIPT = """
    local depth = redis.call('ZCARD', KEYS[1])
    if depth >= tonumber(ARGV[1]) then return 0 end
    redis.call('ZADD', KEYS[1], ARGV[2], ARGV[3])
    return 1
    """
    sha = r.script_load(ENQUEUE_SCRIPT)

    job.enqueued_at = time.time()
    added = r.evalsha(sha, 1, key, max_depth, job.enqueued_at,
                      json.dumps(job.__dict__))
    if not added:
        current_depth = r.zcard(key)
        raise QueueFullError(priority, current_depth)

def test_strict_priority_ordering(fake_redis):
    """
    Enqueue jobs in reverse priority order; verify they are dequeued
    in priority order (critical before batch).
    """
    # Enqueue: batch first, critical last
    for priority in reversed(list(Priority)):
        job = AgentJob.new(priority, "tenant-1", [], [])
        enqueue(job, priority)

    dequeued_priorities = []
    for _ in range(len(Priority)):
        job = next_job(block_timeout=0.1)
        if job:
            dequeued_priorities.append(job.priority)

    assert dequeued_priorities == [
        Priority.CRITICAL.value,
        Priority.HIGH.value,
        Priority.NORMAL.value,
        Priority.BATCH.value,
    ]


def test_aging_promotes_old_batch_job(fake_redis):
    """A very old batch job should be promoted to normal after threshold."""
    job = AgentJob.new(Priority.BATCH, "tenant-1", [], [])
    job.enqueued_at = time.time() - 200  # 200 s ago
    r.zadd(QUEUE_KEY[Priority.BATCH], {json.dumps(job.__dict__): job.enqueued_at})

    promoted = promote_batch_to_normal()
    assert promoted == 1
    assert r.zcard(QUEUE_KEY[Priority.NORMAL]) == 1
    assert r.zcard(QUEUE_KEY[Priority.BATCH])  == 0
on the Redis instance that hosts priority queues — you must never silently drop jobs due to memory pressure. Use back-pressure instead.
(blocking) with a short timeout rather than a polling loop with
— blocking cuts CPU usage by 10–100× on lightly loaded queues.
Monitor queue depth and oldest-job age per tier with a 60-second scrape; alert when oldest-job age exceeds the tier SLA.
Give the aging daemon its own Redis connection separate from the worker pool so a slow aging run does not block
When using per-tenant sub-queues, garbage-collect empty tenant keys to avoid unbounded growth of the tenant set.
Keep the priority tier of a job immutable after enqueue — changing priority on an in-flight job creates ordering races.

Priority queues for agent workloads are a simple but critical reliability pattern. Use one Redis sorted set per tier with the enqueue timestamp as score. Workers poll in strict priority order using BZPOPMIN; combine strict priority with an aging job to prevent batch starvation. Monitor queue depth and oldest-job wait time per tier, and alert when wait time exceeds the tier's SLA. For workloads where batch must always make progress, split workers by tier rather than using strict priority.
